name: Paul Weston
description: American pianist, arranger, composer, and conductor (1912-1996)
abstract: Paul Weston (né Wetstein; March 12, 1912 – September 20, 1996) was an American pianist, arranger, composer, and conductor who worked in music and television from the 1930s to the 1970s, pioneering mood music and becoming known as "the Father of Mood Music". His compositions include popular music songs such as "I Should Care", "Day by Day…

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: Paul Weston came to one of our national meetings and conventions... Paul had come in. He was in charge of recording on the West Coast. I split titles with him. I was the director of popular albums... I suggested, 'Let Paul be East [sic: West] Coast director of popular albums'
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Conniff
about_quotes.evidence: I called all of them and told them, “hey I’m free now, I’m not writing steady all of the time for Harry, I’m available.” And boy, for two years the phone hardly rang. ... like Sonny Burke at Decca and Paul Weston was at Columbia Records.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Conniff interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Bushkin
about_quotes.evidence: Sy Oliver and Alex Stordahl and Paul Weston, we had three great arrangers, and myself, could sketch out a production number
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Bushkin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: We knew about George Van Eps and we know about Allan Reuss... with Paul Weston and all those Sinatra records.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 23, 2003 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Durham
about_quotes.evidence: That's when we all went into Jesse Stone's band... Paul Weston and I, we could play it free.

about_quotes.speaker: Annie Ross
about_quotes.evidence: Paul Weston, who had a great band, who later married – oh, come on – Jo Stafford.
about_quotes.doc_title: Annie Ross — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
